result: Steam rises from my mug and fogs the window, turning morning into a watercolor of smudges. The city outside is waking in degrees: a paper boy throws his route into the street, then vanishes; a cat arches on the stoop, tasting sunlight; a bus exhales and disappears with a sigh. I have a habit of listening from inside the quiet, of noticing who moves first and who still dreams. A woman across the street pauses, sets a small bag down, and ties her shoes. I remember being five and tying mine once in the yard while my grandmother hummed to the wind chimes. That wind sounds the same, even now. A cyclist leans into the hill with a soft, stubborn courage. The sky is the color of an old notebook, inked and honest. Somewhere a saxophone tries out a shy note, practices the art of becoming brave, and somewhere a bakery pours out cinnamon like warm rain. I sip and let the heat teach me its lesson about patience. When I was younger I thought time was a river you could walk along, like it would wait for you to catch up. Now I feel it more like air, everywhere at once, invisible but heavy when you try not to notice. There is a kind of mercy in that. I think of the phone call I didn’t make yesterday and feel the sting, then let it pass. You can’t carry everything and still arrive. The light shifts and so does the angle of my chair, and suddenly a flock of pigeons erupts from the oak, a brief, chaotic punctuation. I laugh and it scares the cat. The woman with the bag checks her watch, stands, and walks away with the economy of someone who knows exactly how far she has to go. I decide to walk too, to the river, where the bridges stretch their patience across slow water and boats cut through the last stubborn fog. On the way I pass the bakery, and the boy who now looks older than his years grins and slides a pastry across the counter for me, no charge, his eyes saying, you look like you could use sweetness. In the park, someone practices tai chi as if the city were a garden, as if there were time for slowness. An old man with a camera watches the birds and then the people, then the birds again, as if they were stories with similar rules. I stand by the railing and watch the river take the sky, a blue stitched into black, then spill both into the night. The sound is the same one that lulled my father when he was a child. The wind moves through the oak trees as if remembering my name. It tells me that beginnings don’t have to announce themselves, that we can become brave in the middle of doing dishes or tying shoes. A child asks me for the time and I say it, and his mother smiles in thanks, a small exchange that feels like currency. I breathe in, breathe out, and return, changed.
